name: L-1
tagline: Transfer key employees from your global office to the United States
description: The L-1 visa allows multinational companies to transfer employees from a foreign affiliate, subsidiary, or parent company to a U.S. office. It is available for managers and executives (L-1A) and employees with specialized knowledge (L-1B). It is also used to establish new U.S. offices for foreign companies expanding into the American market.

requirements: Must have worked for the foreign qualifying organization for at least 1 continuous year within the last 3 years
The U.S. and foreign entities must have a qualifying relationship (parent, subsidiary, affiliate, or branch)
For L-1A: must be coming to work in a managerial or executive capacity
For L-1B: must possess specialized knowledge of the company's products, services, research, systems, or procedures
Both the U.S. and foreign entities must be doing business actively
benefits: L-1A valid for 3 years (new office: 1 year), extendable to 7 years total
L-1B valid for 3 years (new office: 1 year), extendable to 5 years total
No annual cap or lottery — file any time
Dual intent allowed — strong pathway to EB-1C green card for L-1A managers/executives
Spouse and children receive L-2 status; L-2 spouses have automatic work authorization
Can establish a new U.S. office on L-1 — ideal for foreign companies entering the U.S. market

steps.title: Establish qualifying relationship
steps.description: Document the corporate relationship between U.S. and foreign entities (ownership charts, articles of incorporation, financials).

steps.title: Petition filing
steps.description: Employer files Form I-129 with L supplement. Blanket L petitions available for large multinationals.

steps.title: Consular processing or status change
steps.description: Employee obtains L-1 visa stamp at U.S. consulate abroad, or changes status if already in the U.S.

processing.timeline: 1–6 months standard; 15 business days with premium processing
processing.cost: Government fees: $960–$5,460 depending on company size and options. Premium processing: $2,805. Attorney fees additional.
documents: Corporate organizational charts and ownership documentation
Evidence of qualifying relationship between U.S. and foreign entities
Employee's job description and evidence of managerial/executive role or specialized knowledge
Evidence of 1 year continuous employment with foreign entity
For new offices: business plan, office lease, proof of capitalization

faqs.question: What is the difference between L-1A and L-1B?
faqs.answer: L-1A is for managers and executives — those who manage an organization, department, function, or component, or who direct the organization at the highest levels. L-1A can lead to an EB-1C green card (multinational manager/executive) without a PERM labor certification. L-1B is for employees with 'specialized knowledge' — deep knowledge of the company's proprietary products, services, research, or procedures. L-1B does not have the same direct green card pathway.

faqs.question: Can I use L-1 to open a new U.S. office?
faqs.answer: Yes. L-1 is frequently used by foreign companies establishing a U.S. presence. The initial visa for a new office is granted for only 1 year, giving the company time to demonstrate the U.S. office is operational and growing. After 1 year, the visa can be extended for 2-year increments. A business plan and evidence of funding/office space are required.

faqs.question: How does L-1A lead to a green card?
faqs.answer: L-1A managers and executives are eligible for the EB-1C immigrant category, which does not require a PERM labor certification (a lengthy and uncertain process). This is one of the fastest employment-based green card pathways available. To qualify, the person must have been employed as a manager/executive for at least 1 year by the petitioning U.S. employer.

faqs.question: What is a blanket L petition?
faqs.answer: Large multinational companies with a history of L-1 approvals can obtain a 'blanket L' petition from USCIS, which pre-approves the company for L-1 transfers. Individual employees can then obtain L-1 visas at U.S. consulates more quickly by referencing the blanket approval, without filing a separate I-129 for each transfer.

faqs.question: Can my spouse work on L-2 status?
faqs.answer: Yes. L-2 spouses have automatic work authorization incident to their L-2 status — they do not need to file a separate EAD application. This is a significant benefit compared to H-4 dependent spouses, who must apply for H-4 EAD separately.
